metadata.title: Subby: The Substack Writing Tool You Didn't Know You Needed

metadata.note: HAI Fellows batch v2 — Teardown register, @NikBearBrown, Remotion animated bookends. Credit: Nik Bear Brown — Founder, Humanitarians AI. HONESTY: all numbers trace to source article.

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. There's a peculiar gap in writing tools that Subby is built to fill. Its commands adapt structure to purpose while preserving the intellectual depth of the source. Running ezsay on a book title and your notes yields a 1,800 to 2,500 word essay. The output follows a fixed arc: stakes, one reframing digression, a synthesizing return, a resonant close.

artifactLines: There's a peculiar gap in writing tools that Subby is built to fill.
Its commands adapt structure to purpose while preserving the intellectual depth of the source.
Running ezsay on a book title and your notes yields a 1,800 to 2,500 word essay.
The output follows a fixed arc: stakes, one reframing digression, a synthesizing return, a resonant close.

beats.narration_text: The part that intrigued me is a command that adapts structure to purpose while preserving intellectual depth. I want to understand how Subby actually shapes a draft. Walk me through: (1) what the ezsay command does to my book title and notes to produce that 1,800 to 2,500 word arc, (2) how it enforces the opening-stakes, one substantial digression, synthesizing return, and resonant close structure, and (3) how I'd tweak the command so the output reframes my subject instead of just summarizing the book.

beats.narration_text: The intellectual depth is preserved. With Subby: ezsay [book title and your notes]You get 1,800-2,500 words structured exactly as the command specifies: an opening that establishes stakes, one substantial digression that reframes the subject, a return that synthesizes without repeating, and a closing that resonates.
